explicit_text: In October 1940, I was transferred to the 17th Pursuit Squadron and sent to Manila in the Philippine Islands. We trained and prepared for war, eventually flying P-40 Warhawks and facing the Japanese invasion. The squadron was part of the desperate defense during the early days of World War II in the Philippines.

explicit_text: Manila was our initial base when I arrived with the 17th Pursuit Squadron in 1940. It was a strategic location in the Philippine Islands, and we faced intense Japanese attacks there after Pearl Harbor. The city was eventually evacuated, and we were forced to retreat to the Bataan Peninsula.

explicit_text: Our journey to the Philippines began in Detroit, where we boarded trains for deployment. The military kept the train windows covered to avoid alerting anyone about our destination. Detroit was the starting point of a long and secretive trip across the country.

explicit_text: Nichols Field was our main base near Manila where we were stationed before the war broke out. We returned there after training at Iba and faced constant air raid warnings and preparations for attack. It was also where we suffered heavy losses during the Japanese air raids.

explicit_text: From June to October 1941, we were stationed at Iba for air-to-ground gunnery training. It was there that we received our first P-40 Warhawks, which felt like toys to us pilots. Iba was a crucial training ground just before the war erupted in the Pacific.

explicit_text: We learned on December 8th, 1941, that Pearl Harbor had been bombed, which was a turning point for us. Shortly after, Japanese bombers attacked our airfields in the Philippines, including Nichols Field. The Pearl Harbor attack marked the beginning of our direct involvement in World War II combat.

explicit_text: During the initial Japanese air raids on our airfields, Japanese dive bombers attacked our planes as they were landing to refuel. Their precision and aggression were terrifying, and it was my first real experience of war. They caused heavy damage to our aircraft and facilities.

explicit_text: Boyd 'Buzz' Wagner was our commanding officer and a West Point graduate, one of the first aces in World War II. He was blinded in a dogfight over Manila but managed to land safely by radio guidance. His leadership was inspiring during those chaotic early battles.

explicit_text: I served during World War II, starting in 1940 in the Philippines. I witnessed the Japanese invasion, air raids, and the desperate defense of Bataan. The war changed everything for us, from flying P-40s to becoming infantry and enduring the brutal Bataan Death March.

explicit_text: After evacuating Manila, we were stationed on the Bataan Peninsula to defend against the Japanese. Conditions were harsh, with limited food and medical supplies, and we eventually had to surrender. Bataan was a place of intense fighting and suffering during the war.

explicit_text: At Bataan, we had only one P-35 airplane left, which our mechanics desperately kept flying despite its condition. Our lone pilot flew along the coast to gather whatever support he could. The P-35 was outdated but all we had left to try to resist the Japanese advance.

explicit_text: We joined the 71st Philippine Provisional Group, where all personnel, regardless of their original roles, were formed into infantry companies. Quartermasters, cooks, medics, and others were issued rifles and sent into combat. It was a desperate measure reflecting how dire the situation was.

explicit_text: I was issued a .30 caliber Springfield Rifle, made in 1918, when we were turned into infantry on Bataan. It was an old weapon, but it was what we had to defend ourselves with. I carried it along with a bandolier of ammunition during patrols and combat.

explicit_text: On April 8, 1942, we received a message from Corregidor ordering us to surrender the next morning. Corregidor was the last stronghold in the Philippines, and its fall signaled the end of organized resistance on Bataan. The surrender was a moment of relief but also despair.

explicit_text: We were ordered to turn in our weapons at Cabcaben Air Field on Bataan after the surrender. It was a large open space where many units gathered to relinquish arms. The mood was somber as we faced the reality of captivity.

explicit_text: The Japanese Empire was our enemy during the war in the Philippines. Their soldiers captured us after the fall of Bataan, and we endured harsh treatment and brutal marches under their control. They were disciplined but also capable of terrible atrocities.

explicit_text: After surrendering, we were forced to march for days in brutal heat during what became known as the Bataan Death March. Many men died from exhaustion, dehydration, and abuse by Japanese guards. It was one of the darkest experiences of the war for me and my comrades.

explicit_text: During the Bataan Death March, we passed through San Fernando, Pampanga, where prisoners were gathered before being transported further. The conditions were terrible, and the local Filipinos risked their lives to help us by slipping food. San Fernando was a grim waypoint on the march.
